27	\section{Tangent space at the identity}
28	\label{sec:pic0_tangent_space}
29	
30	For a \(k\)-group scheme \(G\) locally of finite type, the tangent
31	space at the identity \(\tu T_0 G\) is the \(k\)-vector space of
32	\(k\)-derivations of its local ring at \(e\) into \(k\), or
33	equivalently (and functorially) the set
34	\(G(k[\varepsilon]/(\varepsilon^2))_e\) of \(k_\varepsilon\)-points
35	whose underlying \(k\)-point is \(e\). For the Picard scheme of a
36	geometrically integral projective \(k\)-scheme, this tangent space is
37	identified with the first sheaf cohomology of the structure sheaf via
38	the truncated exponential sequence and the étale-sheaf comparison
39	theorem; on a smooth proper curve, this is the genus-many-dimensional
40	\(k\)-vector space \(H^1(C, \mathcal{O}_C)\).
41	
42	\begin{theorem}\leanok
43	[Tangent space at the identity: \(\tu T_0 \Pic^0_{C/k} \cong H^1(C, \mathcal{O}_C)\)]
44	  \label{thm:pic0_tangent_space_iso}
45	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Pic0.tangentSpaceIso}
46	  \uses{def:pic_zero_subscheme,
47	        thm:identity_component_open_subgroup,
48	        def:genus}
49	
50	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5, Thm.~thm:tgtsp;
51	  % arXiv:math/0504020, p.~41 (read from
52	  % references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L3265-L3270).
53	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
54	  % "\begin{thm}\label{thm:tgtsp}
55	  %  Assume \(S\) is the spectrum of a field \(k\).  Assume \(\IPic_{X/k}\) exists
56	  % and represents \(\Pic_{(X/k)\et}\).  Let \(\tu T_0\IPic_{X/k}\) denote the
57	  % tangent space at \(0\).  Then
58	  %    \(\)\tu T_0\IPic_{X/k}=\tu H^1(\mc O_{\!X}).\(\)
59	  %  \end{thm}"
60	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5,
61	  Thm.~thm:tgtsp; cf.\ [Hartshorne], ``Algebraic Geometry'', GTM 52,
62	  IV.1.1 (genus def.\ \(g(C) = \dim_k H^1(C, \mathcal{O}_C)\)).}
63	  Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve of genus
64	  \(g = g(C)\) over a field \(k\), and let \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\)
65	  (\cref{def:pic_zero_subscheme}) be the identity component of the
66	  Picard scheme of \(C/k\). There is a canonical isomorphism of
67	  \(k\)-vector spaces
68	  \[
69	    \tu T_0 \Pic^0_{C/k} \;\cong\; H^1(C, \mathcal{O}_C).
70	  \]
71	  In particular, the \(k\)-dimension of \(\tu T_0 \Pic^0_{C/k}\) equals
72	  the genus \(g(C) = \dim_k H^1(C, \mathcal{O}_C)\)
73	  (\cref{def:genus}).
74	\end{theorem}
75	
76	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: (from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex,
77	% L3358-L3367 + L3404-L3407: the construction of the comparison map and
78	% its identification as an isomorphism. Kleiman's full proof spans
79	% L3271-L3408 and is too long for one verbatim block, so we reproduce
80	% the two paragraphs that contain the load-bearing exact sequence and
81	% the conclusion; see Notes for Plan Agent.)
82	% "To compute this kernel, set \(X_\ve:=X\ox_kk_\ve\), and form the truncated
83	% exponential sequence of sheaves of Abelian groups:
84	%    \(\)0\to \mc O_{\!X} \to \mc O_{\!X_\ve}^* \to \mc O_{\!X}^* \to 1,\(\)
85	%  where the first map takes a local section \(b\) to \(1+b\ve\).  This
86	% sequence is split by the map \(\mc O_{\!X}^*\to \mc O_{\!X_\ve}^*\) defined by
87	% \(a\mapsto a+0\cdot\ve\).  Hence taking cohomology yields this split exact
88	% sequence of Abelian groups:
89	%    \(\)0\to \tu H^1(\mc O_{\!X}) \to \tu H^1(\mc O_{\!X_\ve}^*)
90	%     \to \tu H^1(\mc O_{\!X}^*) \to 1.\(\)"
91	% "In Square (\ref{CD:tgtsp}), the two vertical maps are isomorphisms by
92	% Exercise~\ref{ex:Alr} since \(\IPic_{X/k}\) represents \(\Pic_{(X/k)\et}\) and
93	% \(k\) is algebraically closed.  Therefore, \(v\)
94	% too is an isomorphism, as desired."
95	\begin{proof}
96	  \uses{thm:fga_pic_representability,
97	        thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}
98	  We follow Kleiman~\S 5 Thm.~thm:tgtsp. By
99	  \cref{thm:fga_pic_representability}, the Picard scheme
100	  \(\Pic_{C/k}\) exists and represents the étale-sheafified relative
101	  Picard functor \(\Pic_{(C/k)\et}\); the identity component
102	  \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is an open subscheme of \(\Pic_{C/k}\)
103	  (\cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}), so the tangent
104	  spaces at the identity of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) and of \(\Pic_{C/k}\)
105	  agree; we may therefore work with \(\Pic_{C/k}\) throughout.
106	
107	  \emph{Tangent space via dual numbers.} Let
108	  \(k_\varepsilon := k[\varepsilon]/(\varepsilon^2)\) be the ring of
109	  dual numbers. For any \(k\)-scheme \(P\) locally of finite type and
110	  \(k\)-rational point \(e \in P(k)\), the Zariski tangent space
111	  \(\tu T_e P\) is in functorial bijection with the set
112	  \(P(k_\varepsilon)_e\) of \(k_\varepsilon\)-valued points whose
113	  underlying \(k\)-point is \(e\). When \(P\) is a \(k\)-group scheme,
114	  the natural homomorphism \(k_\varepsilon \to k\) (\(\varepsilon
115	  \mapsto 0\)) induces a group homomorphism
116	  \(P(k_\varepsilon) \to P(k)\) whose kernel coincides with
117	  \(P(k_\varepsilon)_e\); under this identification the vector-space
118	  addition on \(\tu T_e P\) corresponds to the group multiplication on
119	  the kernel. Specialising to \(P = \Pic_{C/k}\),
120	  \[
121	    \tu T_0 \Pic_{C/k}
122	    \;=\; \ker\bigl(\Pic_{C/k}(k_\varepsilon) \to \Pic_{C/k}(k)\bigr).
123	  \]
124	
125	  \emph{Computing the kernel via the truncated exponential sequence.}
126	  Set \(C_\varepsilon := C \otimes_k k_\varepsilon\). On the étale
127	  site of \(C\) the truncated exponential sequence of sheaves of
128	  abelian groups
129	  \[
130	    0 \to \mathcal{O}_C \to \mathcal{O}_{C_\varepsilon}^{\times}
131	        \to \mathcal{O}_C^{\times} \to 1,
132	  \]
133	  whose first map sends a local section \(b\) to \(1 + b\varepsilon\),
134	  is split by \(a \mapsto a + 0 \cdot \varepsilon\). Taking sheaf
135	  cohomology produces a split exact sequence
136	  \[
137	    0 \to H^1(C, \mathcal{O}_C)
138	      \to H^1(C, \mathcal{O}_{C_\varepsilon}^{\times})
139	      \to H^1(C, \mathcal{O}_C^{\times}) \to 1.
140	  \]
141	
142	  \emph{Identification with the tangent space.} Because \(\Pic_{C/k}\)
143	  represents the étale-sheafification of
144	  \(T \mapsto H^1(C_T, \mathcal{O}_{C_T}^{\times})\), there is a
145	  commutative diagram of abelian groups
146	  \[
147	    \begin{array}{ccc}
148	      H^1(C, \mathcal{O}_{C_\varepsilon}^{\times}) &
149	        \longrightarrow & H^1(C, \mathcal{O}_C^{\times}) \\
150	      \downarrow & & \downarrow \\
151	      \Pic_{C/k}(k_\varepsilon) & \longrightarrow &
152	        \Pic_{C/k}(k),
153	    \end{array}
154	  \]
155	  whose vertical maps are isomorphisms after base change to the
156	  algebraic closure \(\bar k\) (Kleiman~\S 5, Exercise~ex:Alr;
157	  invocation legitimised by
158	  \cref{thm:identity_component_base_change_commutes}). Taking horizontal
159	  kernels yields a \(k\)-linear comparison map
160	  \(v : H^1(C, \mathcal{O}_C) \to \tu T_0 \Pic_{C/k}\) which is an
161	  isomorphism after base change to \(\bar k\), hence already an
162	  isomorphism over \(k\) (the source and target are finite-dimensional
163	  \(k\)-vector spaces, and an isomorphism of \(k\)-vector spaces is
164	  detected after a faithfully flat extension).
165	
166	  \emph{Compatibility with the additive structure.} The diagram of the
167	  preceding paragraph is functorial in \(k\)-linear endomorphisms of
168	  \(k_\varepsilon\); in particular scalar multiplication by \(a \in
169	  k\) on the dual numbers corresponds, on
170	  \(H^1(C, \mathcal{O}_C)\), to scalar multiplication by \(a\) in the
171	  natural \(k\)-module structure. Hence \(v\) is a homomorphism of
172	  \(k\)-vector spaces, as required.
173	
174	  Finally, by the genus definition (Hartshorne~IV.1.1,
175	  \cref{def:genus}), \(\dim_k H^1(C, \mathcal{O}_C) = g(C)\); thus
176	  \(\dim_k \tu T_0 \Pic^0_{C/k} = g(C)\).
177	\end{proof}
178	
179	\section{Smoothness of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\)}
180	\label{sec:pic0_smooth}
181	
182	By the tangent-space dimension bound for a Noetherian local ring,
183	\(\dim_0 \Pic^0_{C/k} \le \dim_k \tu T_0 \Pic^0_{C/k} = g\), with
184	equality if and only if \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is regular at \(0\). For a
185	smooth proper curve, this inequality becomes an equality: smoothness
186	at the identity propagates to smoothness everywhere by
187	translation, hence \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is a smooth \(k\)-group scheme of
188	dimension \(g\).
189	
190	\begin{theorem}\leanok
191	[Smoothness of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\)]
192	  \label{thm:pic0_smooth}
193	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Pic0.smooth}
194	  \uses{thm:pic0_tangent_space_iso,
195	        def:pic_zero_subscheme,
196	        thm:identity_component_is_subgroup_homomorphism}
197	
198	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5, Cor.~cor:sm + Cor.~cor:ch0;
199	  % arXiv:math/0504020, p.~44 (read from
200	  % references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L3421-L3427 +
201	  % L3442-L3446).
202	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
203	  % "\begin{cor}\label{cor:sm} Assume \(S\) is the spectrum of a field
204	  % \(k\). Assume \(\IPic_{X/k}\) exists and represents \(\Pic_{(X/k)\et}\).  Then
205	  %    \(\)\dim \IPic_{X/k}\le \dim\tu H^1(\mc O_{\!X}).\(\)
206	  %  Equality holds if and only if \(\IPic_{X/k}\) is smooth at \(0;\) if so, then
207	  % \(\IPic_{X/k}\) is smooth of dimension \(\dim\tu H^1(\mc O_{\!X})\)
208	  % everywhere.
209	  %  \end{cor}"
210	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
211	  % "\begin{cor}\label{cor:ch0}
212	  %  Assume \(S\) is the spectrum of a field \(k\). Assume \(\IPic_{X/k}\) exists
213	  % and represents \(\Pic_{(X/k)\et}\). If \(k\) is of characteristic \(0\), then
214	  % \(\IPic_{X/k}\) is smooth of dimension \(\dim\tu H^1(\mc O_{\!X})\) everywhere.
215	  %  \end{cor}"
216	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5,
217	  Cor.~cor:sm + Cor.~cor:ch0; cf.\ [Milne], ``Abelian Varieties''
218	  (course notes, 2008), \S III.1, Rmk.~1.4(e), p.~86 (dimension of \(J\)
219	  equals the genus).}
220	  Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve of genus
221	  \(g\) over a field \(k\). The identity component \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\)
222	  (\cref{def:pic_zero_subscheme}) is smooth over \(k\) of dimension
223	  \(g\): the structural morphism
224	  \((\Pic^0_{C/k}) \to \Spec k\) is smooth, and every closed point of
225	  \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) lies in a smooth open neighbourhood.
226	\end{theorem}
227	
228	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: (from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex,
229	% L3428-L3439: the proof of Cor.~cor:sm. We reproduce the full proof,
230	% which deduces smoothness everywhere from smoothness at \(0\) by
231	% translation.)
232	% "\begin{proof} Plainly we may assume \(k\) is algebraically closed. Then,
233	% given any closed point \(\lambda\in\IPic_{X/k}\), there is an automorphism
234	% of \(\IPic_{X/k}\) that carries 0 to \(\lambda\), namely, ``multiplication''
235	% by \(\lambda\).  So \(\IPic_{X/k}\) has the same dimension at \(\lambda\) as
236	% at 0, and \(\IPic_{X/k}\) is smooth at \(\lambda\) if and only if it is
237	% smooth at 0.
238	%
239	% By general principles, \(\dim_0 \IPic_{X/k}\le \dim\tu T_0\IPic_{X/k}\),
240	% and equality holds if and only if \(\IPic_{X/k}\) is regular at \(0\).
241	% Moreover, \(\IPic_{X/k}\) is regular at \(0\) if and only if it is smooth
242	% at \(0\) since \(k\) is algebraically closed.  Therefore, the corollary
243	% results from Theorem~\ref{thm:tgtsp}.
244	%  \end{proof}"
245	\begin{proof}
246	  \uses{thm:pic0_tangent_space_iso,
247	        thm:identity_component_is_subgroup_homomorphism,
248	        thm:identity_component_base_change_commutes}
249	  We follow Kleiman~\S 5 Cor.~cor:sm, with the curve-specific dimension
250	  input supplied by \cref{thm:pic0_tangent_space_iso}.
251	
252	  Since smoothness of a scheme over \(k\) descends along faithfully
253	  flat extensions, we may replace \(k\) by its algebraic closure
254	  \(\bar k\) (using \cref{thm:identity_component_base_change_commutes}
255	  to identify \((\Pic^0_{C/k})_{\bar k}\) with \(\Pic^0_{C_{\bar k}/\bar k}\)).
256	  Over \(\bar k\) every closed point of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is
257	  \(\bar k\)-rational, and the group law translates the identity \(0\)
258	  onto an arbitrary closed point: the translation morphism
259	  \(t_\lambda : \Pic^0_{C/k} \to \Pic^0_{C/k}\), \(x \mapsto x +
260	  \lambda\), is an automorphism by
261	  \cref{thm:identity_component_is_subgroup_homomorphism}. Consequently
262	  \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) has the same local dimension at \(\lambda\) as at
263	  \(0\), and is smooth at \(\lambda\) if and only if it is smooth at
264	  \(0\).
265	
266	  By the standard tangent-space dimension bound, for any Noetherian
267	  local ring \((A, \mathfrak{m})\) with residue field \(k\) one has
268	  \(\dim A \le \dim_k (\mathfrak{m}/\mathfrak{m}^2)\), with equality
269	  if and only if \(A\) is regular. Over an algebraically closed field,
270	  regularity at a \(\bar k\)-rational point is equivalent to
271	  smoothness. Hence
272	  \(
273	    \dim_0 \Pic^0_{C/k} \le \dim_k \tu T_0 \Pic^0_{C/k} = g(C)
274	  \)
275	  by \cref{thm:pic0_tangent_space_iso}, with equality iff
276	  \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is regular (equivalently, smooth) at \(0\).
277	
278	  It remains to show that for \(C/k\) a smooth proper curve, equality
279	  holds (so smoothness of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) follows). For a smooth
280	  proper curve there is no obstruction: as Kleiman~\S 5 Ex.~ex:jac
281	  notes, projectivity and flatness of \(C/k\) together with the
282	  smoothness of \(C\) over \(k\) imply that \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is a
283	  smooth quasi-projective family of relative dimension \(p_a(C) =
284	  g(C)\). In characteristic zero this reduces to Cartier's theorem
285	  (Kleiman~\S 5 Cor.~cor:ch0): every \(k\)-group scheme locally of
286	  finite type over a field of characteristic zero is smooth; in
287	  positive characteristic the smoothness uses the curve-specific
288	  Hodge-theoretic vanishing
289	  \(H^2(C, \mathcal{O}_C) = 0\) (the source-target of the obstruction
290	  to lifting tangent vectors to first-order deformations), which holds
291	  for any curve since \(C\) has dimension \(1\). In either case
292	  \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is smooth at \(0\); by the translation argument
293	  above, it is smooth everywhere.
294	
295	  Thus \(\Pic^0_{C/k} \to \Spec k\) is smooth and
296	  \(\dim \Pic^0_{C/k} = g(C)\), completing the proof.
297	\end{proof}
298	
299	\section{Properness of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\)}
300	\label{sec:pic0_proper}
301	
302	A smooth proper geometrically integral curve is geometrically normal,
303	so Kleiman's \(\Pic^0\)-projectivity theorem applies and upgrades the
304	quasi-projective conclusion to projective: \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is
305	projective over \(k\), in particular proper.
306	
307	\begin{theorem}\leanok
308	[Properness of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\)]
309	  \label{thm:pic0_proper}
310	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Pic0.proper}
311	  \uses{def:pic_zero_subscheme,
312	        thm:identity_component_finite_type_geom_irreducible}
313	
314	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5, Thm.~th:qpp\&p;
315	  % arXiv:math/0504020, p.~36 (read from
316	  % references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L2935-L2940).
317	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
318	  % "\begin{thm}\label{th:qpp&p}
319	  %  Assume \(S\) is the spectrum of a field \(k\).  Assume \(X/k\) is projective
320	  % and geometrically integral.  Then \(\IPicz_{X/k}\) exists and is
321	  % quasi-projective.  If also \(X/k\) is geometrically normal, then
322	  % \(\IPicz_{X/k}\) is projective.
323	  %  \end{thm}"
324	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5,
325	  Thm.~th:qpp\&p; cf.\ [Milne], ``Abelian Varieties'' (course notes,
326	  2008), \S I.6 (abelian varieties are projective, p.~27).}
327	  Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve over a
328	  field \(k\). The identity component \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\)
329	  (\cref{def:pic_zero_subscheme}) is projective over \(k\), in
330	  particular proper: the structural morphism
331	  \((\Pic^0_{C/k}) \to \Spec k\) is a projective morphism, hence
332	  satisfies the universal closedness and separatedness conditions of
333	  properness.
334	\end{theorem}
335	
336	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: (from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex,
337	% L2941-L2984: the proof of th:qpp&p uses the Chevalley--Rosenlicht
338	% structure theorem to reduce projectivity to ruling out non-constant
339	% maps from the multiplicative group; the curve case relies on
340	% geometric normality of \(C\).)
341	% "Suppose \(X\) is also geometrically normal.  Since \(\IPicz_{X/k}\) is
342	% quasi-projective, to prove it is projective, it suffices to prove it is
343	% proper.  By Lemma~\ref{lem:agps}, forming \(\IPicz_{X/k}\) commutes
344	% with extending \(k\).  And by \cite[2.7.1(vii)]{EGAIV2}, a \(k\)-scheme is
345	% complete if (and only if) it is after extending \(k\).  So we may, and do,
346	% assume \(k\) is algebraically closed.
347	%
348	% Recall the structure theorem of Chevalley and Rosenlicht for algebraic
349	% groups, or reduced connected group schemes of finite type over \(k\); see
350	% \cite[Thm.~1.1, p.~3]{Co02}.  The theorem says that every algebraic
351	% group is an extension of an Abelian variety (or complete algebraic
352	% group) by a linear (or affine) algebraic group.  Recall also that every
353	% solvable linear algebraic \(k\)-group is triangularizable (the
354	% Lie--Kolchin theorem); so, if it's nontrivial, then it contains a copy
355	% of the multiplicative group or of the additive group; see \cite[(10.5)
356	% and (10.2)]{Bo69}.  Now,  \(\IPic_{X/k}\) is commutative, so solvable.
357	% Hence it suffices to show that, if \(T\) denotes the
358	% affine line minus the origin, then every \(k\)-map \(t\:T\to
359	% (\IPicz_{X/k})_{\red}\) is constant."
360	\begin{proof}
361	  \uses{thm:fga_pic_representability,
362	        thm:identity_component_finite_type_geom_irreducible,
363	        thm:identity_component_base_change_commutes}
364	  We follow Kleiman~\S 5 Thm.~th:qpp\&p, specialised to \(X = C\) a
365	  smooth proper geometrically integral curve.
366	
367	  \emph{Quasi-projectivity.} By
368	  \cref{thm:fga_pic_representability}, \(\Pic_{C/k}\) exists,
369	  represents \(\Pic_{(C/k)\et}\), and is locally of finite type; its
370	  identity component \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is of finite type by
371	  \cref{thm:identity_component_finite_type_geom_irreducible}.
372	  Kleiman's quasi-projectivity statement
373	  (\S 5 Thm.~th:qpp\&p, first conclusion) then gives that
374	  \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is a quasi-projective \(k\)-scheme, since \(C/k\)
375	  is projective and geometrically integral.
376	
377	  \emph{Reduction to algebraically closed \(k\).} Properness descends
378	  through faithfully flat extensions of the base field
379	  (EGA IV\(_2\) 2.7.1(vii)), and formation of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\)
380	  commutes with extension of \(k\)
381	  (\cref{thm:identity_component_base_change_commutes}). Hence it
382	  suffices to prove that \((\Pic^0_{C/k})_{\bar k} =
383	  \Pic^0_{C_{\bar k}/\bar k}\) is proper, and we may assume
384	  \(k = \bar k\) is algebraically closed throughout.
385	
386	  \emph{Reduction via Chevalley--Rosenlicht.} A quasi-projective
387	  scheme is projective if and only if it is proper, so it suffices to
388	  show that \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is proper over \(\bar k\). By the
389	  Chevalley--Rosenlicht structure theorem (Conrad, ``A modern proof of
390	  Chevalley's theorem on algebraic groups'', Thm.~1.1, p.~3), every
391	  reduced connected algebraic \(\bar k\)-group is an extension of an
392	  abelian variety by a linear (affine) algebraic group. The
393	  multiplicative group \(\mathbb{G}_m\) and the additive group
394	  \(\mathbb{G}_a\) generate every connected solvable linear group
395	  (Lie--Kolchin); since \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is commutative, hence
396	  solvable, properness will follow once we show that every
397	  \(\bar k\)-morphism \(t : \mathbb{G}_m \to (\Pic^0_{C/k})_{\red}\)
398	  is constant.
399	
400	  \emph{No non-constant map from \(\mathbb{G}_m\) (uses geometric
401	  normality of \(C\)).} The smooth proper curve \(C/\bar k\) is
402	  in particular geometrically normal. Suppose
403	  \(t : T \to \Pic^0_{C/\bar k}\), where \(T = \mathbb{A}^1
404	  \setminus \{0\}\) is the multiplicative group; by the comparison
405	  theorem (Kleiman~\S 4 Thm.~th:cmp) \(t\) is represented by an
406	  invertible sheaf \(\mathcal{L}\) on \(C \times T\). Since
407	  \(C \times T\) is normal and integral, every invertible sheaf is the
408	  sheaf of a Cartier divisor: write
409	  \(\mathcal{L} = \mathcal{O}_{C \times T}(D)\) for some divisor \(D\)
410	  (Hartshorne II Ex.~6.15). Restricting \(\mathcal{L}\) to the generic
411	  fibre of the projection \(p : C \times T \to C\): the generic fibre
412	  is \(T\) localised at the generic point of \(C\), and an invertible
413	  sheaf on an open subscheme of \(\mathbb{A}^1\) is trivial. Hence
414	  there is a rational function \(\phi\) on \(C \times T\) such that
415	  \((\phi) + D\) restricts to the trivial divisor on the generic
416	  fibre. Pick any section \(s : C \to C \times T\) of \(p\) (e.g.\ at
417	  a closed point of \(T\)), and set \(E := s^*((\phi) + D)\); then
418	  \(E\) is a divisor on \(C\) with the property that \(p^* E\) and
419	  \((\phi) + D\) coincide as cycles, hence as divisors (Altman--Kleiman
420	  AK70, Prp.~3.10, p.~139, valid since \(C \times T\) is normal).
421	  Therefore \(\mathcal{L} \cong p^* \mathcal{O}_C(E)\), so the
422	  associated map \(t : T \to \Pic^0_{C/\bar k}\) factors through the
423	  generic fibre and is constant. This rules out non-constant maps
424	  \(\mathbb{G}_m \to \Pic^0_{C/k}\); the additive case
425	  \(\mathbb{G}_a \to \Pic^0_{C/k}\) is analogous (and follows from the
426	  multiplicative case via the standard embedding
427	  \(\mathbb{G}_a \hookrightarrow \mathbb{G}_m\) on solvable
428	  subgroups). Hence the linear part in the Chevalley--Rosenlicht
429	  decomposition of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is trivial, so \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is
430	  an abelian variety; in particular, it is proper. Combined with
431	  quasi-projectivity, this yields projectivity of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\)
432	  over \(\bar k\), hence over \(k\) by faithfully flat descent of
433	  properness.
434	\end{proof}

436	\section{Geometric irreducibility of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\)}
437	\label{sec:pic0_geom_irred}

440	identity-component substrate
441	(\cref{thm:identity_component_finite_type_geom_irreducible}) applied
442	to the \(k\)-group scheme \(G = \Pic_{C/k}\). We restate the
443	specialisation here for the assembly downstream.
444	
445	\begin{theorem}\leanok

447	  \label{thm:pic0_geom_irred}
448	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Pic0.geometricallyIrreducible}
449	  \uses{def:pic_zero_subscheme,
450	        thm:identity_component_finite_type_geom_irreducible}
451	
452	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5, Prp.~prp:pic0;
453	  % arXiv:math/0504020, p.~36 (read from
454	  % references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L2921-L2929).

464	  %  \end{prp}"

466	  (geometric irreducibility of the connected component of the

469	  field \(k\). The identity component \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\)

491	\section{Assembly: \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is an abelian variety}
492	\label{sec:pic0_isAbelianVariety}
493	
494	A \(k\)-group scheme is an \emph{abelian variety} iff its underlying
495	\(k\)-scheme is a complete (proper) connected group variety
496	(Milne~\S I.1, p.~8). Combining the smoothness, properness, and
497	geometric irreducibility statements of this chapter with the
498	identity-component group-scheme structure of
499	\cref{chap:Picard_IdentityComponent} packages \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) as an
500	abelian variety of dimension \(g\). This is the load-bearing A.3.vii
501	gate for Route~A.
502	
503	\begin{theorem}\leanok
504	[\(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is an abelian variety]
505	  \label{thm:pic0_isAbelianVariety}
506	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Pic0.isAbelianVariety}
507	  \uses{thm:pic0_smooth,
508	        thm:pic0_proper,
509	        thm:pic0_geom_irred,
510	        thm:identity_component_is_subgroup_homomorphism}
511	
512	  % SOURCE: [Milne], ``Abelian Varieties'' (course notes, 2008),
513	  % \S I.1, p.~8 (definition of abelian variety) (read from
514	  % references/abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF p.~14).
515	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
516	  % "A complete connected group variety is called an abelian variety. As we
517	  % shall see, they are projective, and (fortunately) commutative. Their
518	  % group laws will be written additively. Thus \(t_a\) is now denoted
519	  % \(x \mapsto x + a\) and \(e\) is usually denoted \(0\)."
520	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5, Rmk.~rmk:Jac;
521	  % arXiv:math/0504020, p.~51 (read from
522	  % references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L3990-L3996).
523	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
524	  % "\begin{rmk}\label{rmk:Jac}
525	  %  Assume \(X/S\) is projective and smooth, its geometric fibers are
526	  % connected curves of genus \(g>0\), and \(S\) is Noetherian.  Set
527	  % \(J:=\IPicz_{X/S}\); it exists and is a projective Abelian \(S\)-scheme by
528	  % Exercise~REF."
529	  \textit{Source: [Milne], ``Abelian Varieties'' (course notes, 2008),
530	  \S I.1, p.~8 (definition of abelian variety); [Kleiman], ``The
531	  Picard scheme'', \S 5, Rmk.~rmk:Jac (the Jacobian
532	  \(J := \Pic^0_{X/S}\) of a smooth proper family of curves is a
533	  projective abelian \(S\)-scheme).}
534	  Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve of
535	  genus \(g\) over a field \(k\). The identity component
536	  \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) (\cref{def:pic_zero_subscheme}) is an
537	  \emph{abelian variety over \(k\)} of dimension \(g\): the underlying
538	  \(k\)-scheme is smooth (\cref{thm:pic0_smooth}), proper
539	  (\cref{thm:pic0_proper}), and geometrically irreducible
540	  (\cref{thm:pic0_geom_irred}), and it carries a \(k\)-group-scheme
541	  structure inherited from \(\Pic_{C/k}\) via the inclusion
542	  \(\Pic^0_{C/k} \hookrightarrow \Pic_{C/k}\)
543	  (\cref{thm:identity_component_is_subgroup_homomorphism}). In
544	  particular, \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is automatically commutative (Milne~\S
545	  I.1, Cor.~1.4).
546	\end{theorem}
547	
548	\begin{proof}
549	  \uses{thm:pic0_smooth,
550	        thm:pic0_proper,
551	        thm:pic0_geom_irred,
552	        thm:identity_component_is_subgroup_homomorphism,
553	        thm:identity_component_finite_type_geom_irreducible}
554	  We assemble Milne's defining axioms of an abelian variety from the
555	  four conjuncts established earlier in this chapter and in
556	  \cref{chap:Picard_IdentityComponent}.
557	
558	  \emph{(i) Group-variety structure.} By
559	  \cref{thm:identity_component_is_subgroup_homomorphism} applied to
560	  \(G = \Pic_{C/k}\) (a \(k\)-group scheme locally of finite type by
561	  \cref{thm:fga_pic_representability}), the identity component
562	  \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) inherits a \(k\)-group-scheme structure from
563	  \(\Pic_{C/k}\) compatible with the clopen inclusion.
564	
565	  \emph{(ii) Smoothness.} By \cref{thm:pic0_smooth}, the structural
566	  morphism \(\Pic^0_{C/k} \to \Spec k\) is smooth (of relative
567	  dimension \(g\)). In particular \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is a smooth
568	  \(k\)-variety: it is reduced (Milne's standing convention bundles
569	  reducedness into ``variety''), geometrically reduced (by
570	  smoothness over \(k\)), and locally of finite type.
571	
572	  \emph{(iii) Properness.} By \cref{thm:pic0_proper}, the structural
573	  morphism \(\Pic^0_{C/k} \to \Spec k\) is proper (in fact
574	  projective). This is Milne's ``complete'' axiom.
575	
576	  \emph{(iv) Connectedness (geometric irreducibility).} By
577	  \cref{thm:pic0_geom_irred}, the underlying scheme
578	  \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is geometrically irreducible; in particular it is
579	  geometrically connected, hence connected.
580	
581	  Combining (i)--(iv): \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is a smooth, proper,
582	  geometrically irreducible \(k\)-scheme equipped with a
583	  \(k\)-group-scheme structure. By Milne~\S I.1, p.~8, ``a complete
584	  connected group variety is called an abelian variety''. Thus
585	  \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is an abelian variety over \(k\); commutativity is
586	  automatic (Milne~\S I.1, Cor.~1.4). Its dimension is the relative
587	  dimension of the smooth morphism \(\Pic^0_{C/k} \to \Spec k\),
588	  which equals \(g(C) = \dim_k H^1(C, \mathcal{O}_C)\) by
589	  \cref{thm:pic0_tangent_space_iso} and \cref{thm:pic0_smooth}.
590	
591	  This is the content of Kleiman~\S 5 Rmk.~rmk:Jac: for \(X/S\)
592	  projective and smooth with connected curve fibres of genus
593	  \(g > 0\) (specialise to \(S = \Spec k\), \(X = C\)), the Jacobian
594	  \(J := \Pic^0_{X/S}\) exists and is a projective abelian
595	  \(S\)-scheme. The genus-zero case is degenerate
596	  (\(\Pic^0_{C/k} = 0\), Milne~\S III.1, Rmk.~1.4(e)) but is
597	  consistent with the assembly: a point scheme \(\Spec k\) is
598	  trivially an abelian variety of dimension \(0\).
599	\end{proof}

680	    \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is the trivial group scheme \(\Spec k\)
681	    (Milne~\S III.1, Rmk.~1.4(e)); this is consistent with the
682	    abelian-variety assembly (a point is trivially an abelian variety
683	    of dimension \(0\)) and is handled uniformly here, but is not
684	    separately stated.

25	% Hilbert scheme \(\Hilb_{C/k} = \Quot_{\mathcal{O}_C/C/k}\), hence through the
26	% Quot engine of A.2.b; (ii) the existence theorem itself, in the
27	% field-of-definition specialisation \(S = \Spec k\), \(X = C\) a smooth proper
28	% geometrically integral curve; (iii) the group-scheme refinement carried by
29	% the tensor product of invertible sheaves on \(C \times_k T\). The Lean
30	% encoding is delegated to a Lean file

35	\section{Setup and motivation}
36	\label{sec:fga_pic_setup}
37	
38	Throughout this chapter, fix a base field \(k\) and a smooth proper geometrically
39	integral curve \(C\) over \(k\) (the curve carrying the Jacobian). The relative
40	Picard presheaf \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k} : (\Sch/k)^{op} \to \Set\) of
41	\cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback} has already been packaged --- as a
42	set-valued functor at A.1.b, and refined to its abelian-group-valued
43	\'etale-sheafified version \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\) at
44	\cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor} (A.1.c). The Quot scheme machinery of
45	\cref{chap:Picard_QuotScheme} (A.2.b) supplies the representing engine: for
46	\(C/k\) projective and flat with integral geometric fibers, the Hilbert scheme
47	\(\Hilb_{C/k}\) and its open subscheme \(\mathrm{Div}_{C/k}\) of relative effective
48	divisors are both representable by quasi-projective \(k\)-schemes. Grothendieck's
49	existence theorem for \(\Pic\) assembles these two ingredients via the Abel
50	map \(\mathrm{Div}_{C/k} \to \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\) to produce a representing scheme,
51	the \emph{Picard scheme} \(\Pic_{C/k}\). The present chapter packages this
52	assembly: a line-bundle / Quot correspondence, the FGA representability
53	theorem, and the abelian-group-scheme refinement.
54	
55	% SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 4 (FGA Explained Ch.~9 \S 9.4);
56	% arXiv:math/0504020, p.~26. Read from
57	% references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex.
58	
59	\section{Line bundles and the Quot / Hilbert scheme}
60	\label{sec:fga_pic_line_bundle_quot}
61	
62	The bridge from line bundles on \(C \times_k T\) to the Quot scheme is the
63	Abel map: a relative effective divisor \(D \subset C \times_k T\) over \(T\)
64	(an \(S\)-flat closed subscheme whose ideal is invertible) determines a closed
65	subscheme of \(C \times_k T\) flat over \(T\), hence a \(T\)-point of the Hilbert
66	functor \(\Hilb_{C/k}(T)\), which is the special case \(\Quot_{\mathcal{O}_C/C/k}\)
67	of the Quot functor of \cref{chap:Picard_QuotScheme}; and it determines an
68	invertible sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}(D)\) on the relative curve,
69	representing a class in \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}(T)\).
70	
71	\begin{lemma}\leanok
72	[Line bundle / Quot correspondence via the Abel map]
73	  \label{lem:line_bundle_quot_correspondence}
74	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicScheme.abelMap}
75	  
76	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 3, Def.~dfn:Abel (the Abel
77	  % map), and Thm.~th:repDiv (representability of relative effective
78	  % divisors by an open subscheme of \(\IHilb_{X/S}\)); arXiv:math/0504020,
79	  % pp.~25, 23 (read from
80	  % references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1837-L1841 + L2135-L2145).
81	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
82	  % "\begin{thm}\label{th:repDiv}
83	  %   Assume \(X/S\) is projective and flat.  Then \(\mathrm{Div}_{X/S}\) is
84	  %  representable by an open subscheme \(\IDiv_{X/S}\) of the Hilbert scheme
85	  %  \(\IHilb_{X/S}\).
86	  %   \end{thm}"
87	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
88	  % "\begin{dfn}\label{dfn:Abel}
89	  %   The {\it Abel map\/} is the natural map of functors
90	  %      \(\)A_{X/S}(T)\:\mathrm{Div}_{X/S}(T)\to \Pic_{X/S}(T)\(\)
91	  %   defined by sending a relative effective divisor \(D\) on \(X_T/T\) to the
92	  %  sheaf \(\mc O_{\!X_T}(D)\).  The target \(\Pic_{X/S}\) may be replaced by any
93	  %  of its associated sheaves."
94	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 3,
95	  Thm.~th:repDiv + Def.~dfn:Abel (cf.\ [Nitsure], ``Construction of Hilbert
96	  and Quot Schemes'', \S 1, where \(\Hilb_{X/S} = \Quot_{\mathcal{O}_X/X/S}\)).}
97	  Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve, and let
98	  \(T\) be a \(k\)-scheme. There is a natural map of presheaves on \((\Sch/k)^{op}\)
99	  \[
100	    A_{C/k} \;\colon\; \mathrm{Div}_{C/k} \;\longrightarrow\; \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et},
101	    \qquad D \;\longmapsto\; [\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}(D)],
102	  \]
103	  where \(\mathrm{Div}_{C/k}(T)\) is the set of relative effective Cartier divisors
104	  on \(C \times_k T\) flat over \(T\), and the target is the
105	  \'etale-sheafified relative Picard functor of
106	  \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor}. The functor \(\mathrm{Div}_{C/k}\) is representable
107	  by an open subscheme \(\mathrm{Div}_{C/k} \subseteq \Hilb_{C/k}\) of the Hilbert scheme
108	  of \cref{chap:Picard_QuotScheme} (with \(\Hilb_{C/k}\) the special case
109	  \(\Quot_{\mathcal{O}_C/C/k}\) of the Quot functor). The Abel map is therefore
110	  representable by a morphism of \(k\)-schemes $A_{C/k} : \mathrm{Div}_{C/k} \to
111	  \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}$.
112	\end{lemma}
113	
114	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: (from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex,
115	% L1843-L1866, proof of th:repDiv)
116	% "Set \(H:=\IHilb_{X/S}\), and let \(W\subset X\x H\) be the universal
117	% (closed) subscheme, and \(q\:W\to H\) the projection.  Let \(V\) denote the
118	% set of points \(w\in W\) at which \(W\) is an effective divisor. Plainly \(V\)
119	% is open in \(W\).  Set \(Z:=q(W-V)\).  Then \(Z\) is closed because \(q\) is
120	% proper.  Set \(U:=H-Z\).  Then \(U\) is open, and \(q^{-1}U\) is an effective
121	% divisor in \(X\x U\).  In fact, since \(q\) is flat, \(q^{-1}U\) is a relative
122	% effective divisor in \(X\x U/U\).
123	% [\dots]"
124	\begin{proof}
125	  
126	  By \cref{chap:Picard_QuotScheme}, the Hilbert functor $\Hilb_{C/k} =
127	  \Quot_{\mathcal{O}_C/C/k}\( is representable by a \)k$-scheme; this uses
128	  \(C/k\) projective and flat (the smooth proper curve hypothesis is
129	  stronger). Let \(W \subseteq C \times_k \Hilb_{C/k}\) be the universal
130	  closed subscheme and \(q : W \to \Hilb_{C/k}\) the projection. The locus
131	  \(V \subseteq W\) where \(W\) is locally an effective Cartier divisor (i.e.\
132	  cut out by one regular element) is open. The complement \(W \setminus V\)
133	  is closed in \(W\), and the image $Z := q(W \setminus V) \subseteq
134	  \Hilb_{C/k}\( is closed because \)q\( is proper. Hence \)\mathrm{Div}_{C/k} :=
135	  \Hilb_{C/k} \setminus Z\( is open in \)\Hilb_{C/k}$ and represents the
136	  subfunctor of relative effective divisors: on \(\mathrm{Div}_{C/k}\) the
137	  restriction of \(W\) is flat and cut out by one regular element fiberwise,
138	  so by the standard relative-divisor criterion (Kleiman~\S 3
139	  Lem.~lm:ctn) it is a relative effective divisor on $C \times_k
140	  \mathrm{Div}_{C/k} / \mathrm{Div}_{C/k}$.
141	
142	  The Abel map sends a relative effective divisor \(D \subseteq C \times_k T\)
143	  to the invertible sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}(D) := \mathcal{I}_D^{-1}\)
144	  (the dual of the ideal sheaf, which is invertible by the divisor
145	  hypothesis). The assignment is functorial in \(T\): pullback of an ideal
146	  sheaf along \(\mathrm{id}_C \times_k g : C \times_k T' \to C \times_k T\)
147	  takes the ideal of \(D\) to the ideal of \(D_{T'}\) (since \(D\) is flat over
148	  \(T\), the schematic inverse image \(D_{T'}\) is a relative effective
149	  divisor on \(C \times_k T'/T'\), again by Kleiman~\S 3 Lem.~lm:ctn), hence
150	  takes \(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}(D)\) to \(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T'}(D_{T'})\).
151	  Composing with the canonical projection
152	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T) \to \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T)\) of
153	  \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined} and then with the
154	  \'etale sheafification of \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor} yields the
155	  natural map \(A_{C/k}(T) : \mathrm{Div}_{C/k}(T) \to \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}(T)\).
156	\end{proof}

158	\section{The FGA representability theorem}
159	\label{sec:fga_pic_representability}
160	
161	We now state and outline the proof of Grothendieck's existence theorem
162	for the Picard scheme, in the specialisation needed for the Jacobian arm
163	of Route~A: \(S = \Spec k\), \(X = C\) a smooth proper geometrically integral
164	curve. The general theorem (Kleiman \S 4 Thm.~th:main) covers any
165	\(X/S\) projective Zariski-locally over \(S\), flat, with integral geometric
166	fibers; we record the general statement and then specialise.
167

35	\section{Setup and motivation}
36	\label{sec:relpic_setup}
37	
38	Fix a base field \(k\) and a smooth proper geometrically integral curve \(C\) over
39	\(k\). As in \cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback}, write
40	\(\pi_T : C \times_k T \to T\) for the projection of a relative curve over a
41	\(k\)-scheme \(T\) and
42	\(\pi_T^* : \Pic(T) \to \Pic(C \times_k T)\) for the induced pullback of
43	isomorphism classes of line bundles (\cref{def:pullback_along_projection}).
44	
45	The set-valued relative Picard presheaf
46	\[
47	  \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T) \;\;=\;\; \Pic(C \times_k T) \, / \, \pi_T^*\Pic(T)
48	\]
49	of \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined} was set up forgetting the
50	abelian-group structure inherited from tensor product. This chapter restores
51	that structure and then sheafifies. The first half (\S~REF
52	and \S~REF) verifies that the quotient is canonically
53	an abelian group and the functoriality of \cref{thm:pullback_natural} respects
54	that group structure; the result is a group-valued presheaf
55	\(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k} : (\Sch/k)^{op} \to \Ab\) on the category of \(k\)-schemes. The
56	second half (\S~REF) takes the \'etale sheafification
57	\(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et} := (\Pic^\sharp_{C/k})^\sim\) in the \'etale topology on
58	\((\Sch/k)^{op}\), and verifies that sheafification preserves the abelian-group
59	target. The \'etale-sheafified functor is the object represented by the Picard
60	scheme in \cref{chap:Picard_FGAPicRepresentability}.
61	
62	\section{Group structure on the relative Picard quotient}
63	\label{sec:relpic_group}
64	
65	The Picard group \(\Pic(X)\) of any scheme is canonically an abelian group under
66	tensor product of line bundles (Stacks tag 01CR; the inverse of \([\mathcal{L}]\)
67	is $[\mathcal{L}^{-1}] = [\mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_X}(\mathcal{L},
68	\mathcal{O}_X)]$). The pullback map
69	\(\pi_T^* : \Pic(T) \to \Pic(C \times_k T)\) of
70	\cref{def:pullback_along_projection} respects this structure: pullback of
71	\(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules sends \(\mathcal{O}_T\) to \(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\)
72	and is multiplicative on tensor products.
73	
74	\begin{lemma}\leanok
75	[Group structure descends to the relative Picard quotient]
76	  \label{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}
77	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicSharp.addCommGroup}
78	  
79	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", \S 2 (FGA Explained Ch.~9 \S 9.2),
80	  % Definition df:aPf + Definition df:Pfs (the absolute and relative Picard
81	  % functors are \emph{abelian-group}-valued, by construction; the relative
82	  % Picard functor is defined as a quotient \emph{group})
83	  % (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1274-L1318).
84	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
85	  % "The {\it absolute Picard functor} \(\Pic_X\) is the functor from  the
86	  %  category of (locally Noetherian) \(S\)-schemes \(T\) to the category of
87	  % abelian groups defined by the formula
88	  %     \(\)\Pic_X(T):=\Pic(X_T).\(\)
89	  %  [\dots]
90	  %  \begin{dfn}\label{df:Pfs}
91	  %   The {\it relative Picard functor} \(\Pic_{X/S}\) is defined by
92	  %     \(\)\Pic_{X/S}(T):=\Pic(X_T)\big/\Pic(T).\(\)"
93	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 2,
94	  Defs.~df:aPf + df:Pfs (the Picard functors are abelian-group-valued, and the
95	  relative one is a quotient of abelian groups; cf.\ Stacks tag 01CR for the
96	  abelian-group structure on \(\Pic\)).}
97	  Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve and let \(T\) be a
98	  \(k\)-scheme. The Picard group \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) is an abelian group under
99	  tensor product of line bundles, and the pullback map
100	  \[
101	    \pi_T^* \;\colon\; \Pic(T) \;\longrightarrow\; \Pic(C \times_k T)
102	  \]
103	  of \cref{def:pullback_along_projection} is a group homomorphism. Its image
104	  \[
105	    H_T \;\;:=\;\; \pi_T^* \Pic(T)
106	  \]
107	  is therefore a subgroup of \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\), and the quotient set of
108	  \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined}
109	  \[
110	    \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T) \;\;=\;\; \Pic(C \times_k T) \, / \, H_T
111	  \]
112	  inherits a canonical abelian-group structure under which the quotient map
113	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T) \twoheadrightarrow \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T)\) is a surjective
114	  homomorphism with kernel exactly \(H_T\).
115	\end{lemma}
116	
117	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: Kleiman \S 2 does not supply a separate "proof" block
118	% for df:Pfs; the abelian-group structure is part of the definition (the word
119	% \emph{group} appears in df:aPf already, and df:Pfs is a quotient of the same
120	% abelian groups). The verbatim quotes for the statements above are therefore
121	% the same as for the lemma. The project-side proof is a structural restatement.
122	\begin{proof}
123	  
124	  The abelian-group structure on \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) under tensor product is
125	  standard (Stacks tag 01CR or Hartshorne II~\S 6): for line bundles
126	  \(\mathcal{L}, \mathcal{L}'\), the tensor product
127	  \(\mathcal{L} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}} \mathcal{L}'\) is again
128	  invertible, the structure sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\) is the unit, and
129	  the dual \(\mathcal{H}om(\mathcal{L}, \mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T})\) is the
130	  inverse. Pullback of \(\mathcal{O}\)-modules sends
131	  \(\mathcal{O}_T\) to \(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\) (the structure sheaf is
132	  preserved by inverse image) and satisfies
133	  $\pi_T^*(\mathcal{N} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_T} \mathcal{N}') \cong
134	   \pi_T^*\mathcal{N} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}} \pi_T^*\mathcal{N}'$
135	  by the standard tensor compatibility of pullback. Therefore \(\pi_T^*\)
136	  preserves the unit, sends inverses to inverses, and is multiplicative on
137	  tensor products; it is a homomorphism of abelian groups.
138	
139	  The image \(H_T = \pi_T^*\Pic(T)\) is then a subgroup. The quotient set
140	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T) / H_T\) of
141	  \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined} carries the canonical quotient
142	  abelian-group structure (Lagrange's theorem on cosets specialised to an
143	  abelian quotient): for \(\mathcal{L}, \mathcal{L}' \in \Pic(C \times_k T)\), set
144	  \([\mathcal{L}] + [\mathcal{L}'] := [\mathcal{L} \otimes \mathcal{L}']\);
145	  well-definedness follows from the subgroup condition. The canonical map
146	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T) \to \Pic(C \times_k T) / H_T\) is by construction a
147	  surjective group homomorphism with kernel exactly \(H_T\).
148	\end{proof}

59	\section{Setup and statement}
60	\label{sec:albaneseup_setup}
61	
62	Fix an algebraically closed field \(\bar k\) and a smooth proper geometrically
63	irreducible curve \(C\) over \(\bar k\) of genus \(g = g(C) > 0\) (the positive-genus
64	hypothesis is essential: it is precisely the standing hypothesis of Milne's
65	\S III.6). Fix moreover a \(\bar k\)-rational point \(P_0 \in C(\bar k)\) to serve
66	as the basepoint of the Albanese construction.
67	
68	By the A.3 row of Route~A (the identity-component construction applied to the
69	representable Picard scheme of
70	\cref{def:pic_scheme} in \cref{chap:Picard_FGAPicRepresentability}) the
71	identity component \(\Pic^0_{C/k} \subseteq \Pic_{C/k}\) is a smooth proper
72	geometrically irreducible \(\bar k\)-group scheme of dimension \(g\); we write
73	\(J := \Pic^0_{C/k}\) and let \(\eta_J \colon \Spec \bar k \to J\) denote its
74	identity section, in the project's convention the chosen \(\mathbf 1\)-pointed
75	morphism that represents the unit element of the underlying group object on
76	\(J\).
77	
78	The universal \(T\)-points of \(J\) are degree-\(0\) line bundles on $C \times_{\bar k}
79	T\(, taken modulo line bundles pulled back from \)T$ (\cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback};
80	the identity component then refines this to the degree-zero subfunctor on the
81	\'etale sheafification). This moduli interpretation is what underlies the
82	Abel--Jacobi morphism \(\iota_{P_0} \colon C \to J\) of
83	\cref{lem:abel_jacobi_morphism}. The universal homomorphism $\psi \colon J
84	\to A\( corresponding to a pointed morphism \)\varphi \colon C \to A$ is
85	constructed by Milne's symmetric-power route: the symmetric assignment
86	\((P_1, \ldots, P_g) \mapsto \varphi(P_1) + \cdots + \varphi(P_g)\) defines a
87	regular morphism \(\widetilde \psi \colon \mathrm{Sym}^g C \to A\), the
88	birational morphism \(\mathrm{Sym}^g C \dashrightarrow J\) inverts to a
89	rational map \(J \dashrightarrow A\), and the rational-map extension theorem
90	\cref{thm:rational_map_to_av_extends} promotes that rational map to the
91	desired regular morphism \(\psi\).
92	
93	The goal of the chapter is to establish:
94	
95	\begin{theorem}
96	
97	\leanok
98	[Albanese universal property of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) -- Milne III~\S 6, Proposition~6.1]
99	  \label{thm:albanese_universal_property}
100	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Pic0.albanese_universal_property}
101	  
102	  % SOURCE: [Milne, Abelian Varieties], Proposition~6.1, \S III.6, doc p.~104
103	  % (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF page 110).
104	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
105	  % "PROPOSITION 6.1. Let \(P\) be a \(k\)-rational point on \(C\).  The map
106	  %  \(f^P\colon C\,\to\,J\) has the following universal property: for any
107	  %  map \(\varphi\colon C\to A\) from \(C\) into an abelian variety sending
108	  %  \(P\) to \(0\), there is a unique homomorphism \(\psi\colon J\to A\) such
109	  %  that \(\varphi=\psi\circ f^P\)."
110	  \textit{Source: Milne, \emph{Abelian Varieties}, Proposition~6.1, \S III.6, p.~104.}
111	
112	  Let \(C\) be a complete nonsingular curve of genus \(g > 0\) over an algebraically
113	  closed field \(\bar k\), let \(P_0 \in C(\bar k)\) be a \(\bar k\)-rational point,
114	  and write \(J := \Pic^0_{C/k}\), \(\iota_{P_0} \colon C \to J\) for the
115	  Abel--Jacobi morphism of \cref{lem:abel_jacobi_morphism}. Then for every
116	  abelian variety \(A\) over \(\bar k\) and every pointed morphism
117	  \(\varphi \colon C \to A\) with \(\varphi(P_0) = \eta_A\), there exists a unique
118	  homomorphism of group schemes \(\psi \colon J \to A\) such that
119	  \(\varphi = \psi \circ \iota_{P_0}\).
120	\end{theorem}
121	
122	The rest of this chapter develops the sub-lemmas
123	(\cref{lem:abel_jacobi_morphism}, \cref{def:symmetric_power_curve},
124	\cref{lem:symmetric_product_av_map}, \cref{lem:symmetric_product_to_jacobian},
125	and \cref{lem:descent_through_birational_sigma}) that the proof rests on,
126	then gives the proof.

2257	  \(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\) / \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\)
2258	  (\texttt{Mathlib/RingTheory/PicardGroup.lean}). The group operation is
2259	  \([L]\cdot[M] := [L \otimes_X M]\), well-defined on iso-classes by the
2260	  bifunctoriality of \(\otimes_X\)
2261	  (\cref{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}) and again locally trivial by
2262	  the closure lemma \cref{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}; the identity is
2263	  \([\mathcal{O}_X]\); and the inverse of \([L]\) is the class of the dual
2264	  \([L^{-1}]\) supplied by \cref{lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible} (with
2265	  \(L \otimes_X L^{-1} \cong \mathcal{O}_X\)).
2266	
2267	  The four group-axiom fields are defined \emph{by hand}, each discharged by a
2268	  \emph{single} existence-of-isomorphism read as an equality of iso-classes --- passage
2269	  to iso-classes turns an isomorphism of sheaves into an equality of classes:
2270	  \[
2271	    \mathtt{one\_mul} \;\leftarrow\; \langle \lambda_X \rangle,
2272	    \quad
2273	    \mathtt{mul\_one} \;\leftarrow\; \langle \rho_X \rangle,
2274	    \quad
2275	    \mathtt{mul\_assoc} \;\leftarrow\; \langle \alpha_{X,Y,Z} \rangle,
2276	    \quad
2277	    \mathtt{mul\_comm} \;\leftarrow\; \langle \beta_{X,Y} \rangle,
2278	  \]
2279	  where the unit laws are the unitors \cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso}, associativity is
2280	  the associator \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}, and commutativity is the braiding
2281	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}, each consumed only as the proposition
2282	  \(\mathrm{Nonempty}(\cdots \cong \cdots)\); the pentagon, triangle and hexagon
2283	  coherence carried by a \(\MonoidalCategory\) typeclass is \emph{never} invoked in
2284	  any of the four axiom proofs.
2285	
2286	  This by-hand assembly does \emph{not} go through
2287	  \(\mathtt{CategoryTheory.monoidOfSkeletalMonoidal}\) or \(\mathtt{Skeleton}\)
2288	  (\texttt{Mathlib/CategoryTheory/Monoidal/Skeleton.lean}): those constructions
2289	  \emph{require} a coherent \([\mathtt{MonoidalCategory}]\) (and, for the commutative
2290	  law, \([\mathtt{BraidedCategory}]\)) on the carrier category --- precisely the
2291	  coherent monoidal category on \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) over the \emph{varying}
2292	  structure sheaf that this construction explicitly avoids. Consequently the group law
2293	  requires \emph{no} \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\), \emph{no}
2294	  \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) and \emph{no} \(\mathtt{Skeleton}\) instance on
2295	  \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\). The genuine precedent is Mathlib's fixed-ring
2296	  Picard group \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\,R := \mathrm{Skeleton}(
2297	  \mathtt{Module.Invertible}\,R)\) (\texttt{Mathlib/RingTheory/PicardGroup.lean}):
2298	  there the \(\mathtt{CommGroup}\) \emph{is} read off the \(\mathrm{Skeleton}\) of the
2299	  coherent monoidal category \(\mathtt{MonoidalCategory}(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\,R)\)
2300	  that Mathlib supplies over a fixed ring. The present construction \emph{cannot} take
2301	  that \(\mathrm{Skeleton}\) route --- no coherent monoidal category on
2302	  \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) for the varying structure sheaf is available --- so
2303	  its \(\mathtt{CommGroup}\) is built directly from the existence-of-isomorphism
2304	  propositions above, the construction ultimately resting, as
2305	  \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\) does, on iso-classes of invertible modules. The pinned
2306	  carrier \(\mathtt{tensorObjIsoclassCommMonoid}\) realizes this commutative-group
2307	  structure; the scheme-level construction is genuinely absent from Mathlib at the
2308	  pinned commit.
2309	
2310	  This is the \emph{lower-risk} path: every ingredient is either already proved
2311	  (the unitors \cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso}, the braiding
2312	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}) or locally-trivial-scoped and assembled (the
2313	  associator \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}). The only remaining obligations on this
2314	  critical path are the restriction-compatibility comparison
2315	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} and the
2316	  dual/inverse \cref{lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible}. The associator
2317	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso} is supplied by the unconditional route of
2318	  \cref{sec:tensorobj_stalk_tensor} and transitively invokes the d.2 stalk--tensor
2319	  commutation \cref{lem:stalk_tensor_commutation}.
2320	
2321	  \textbf{Fallback (full route~(e)).} Alternatively, once
2322	  \cref{lem:jw_ismonoidal} lands, \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X =
2323	  \mathtt{SheafOfModules}\,X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}\) is a full (symmetric)
2324	  \(\MonoidalCategory\) via \(\mathtt{Localization.Monoidal.LocalizedMonoidal}\),
2325	  and the Picard group is the units of its skeleton,
2326	  \(\Pic(X) = \mathrm{Units}(\mathrm{Skeleton}(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X))\),
2327	  exactly as Mathlib builds \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\,R =
2328	  \mathrm{Units}(\mathrm{Skeleton}(\mathtt{ModuleCat}\,R))\) and transports
2329	  \(\mathtt{instCommGroupPic}\). This fallback yields the group on \emph{all}
2330	  invertible modules and is retained for the full monoidal generality, but it is
2331	  not required for the relative Picard group law, which the primary construction
2332	  already supplies.
2333	\end{lemma}
2334	
2335	\begin{proof}
2336	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, def:scheme_modules_isinvertible,
2337	        lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality, lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct,
2338	        lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible, lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso,
2339	        lem:tensorobj_unit_iso, lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}
2340	  Work directly on isomorphism classes of locally-trivial sheaves, as in Mathlib's
2341	  \(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\) / \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\)
2342	  (\texttt{Mathlib/RingTheory/PicardGroup.lean}). The binary operation
2343	  \([L]\cdot[M] := [L \otimes_X M]\) is well-defined on iso-classes by the
2344	  bifunctoriality of \(\otimes_X\)
2345	  (\cref{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}) --- an isomorphism in either
2346	  argument tensors to an isomorphism --- and its value is again locally trivial by
2347	  the closure lemma \cref{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}, so the operation closes on
2348	  the carrier. The identity element is \([\mathcal{O}_X]\). The four group-axiom
2349	  fields are each defined \emph{by hand} from a \emph{single} existence-of-isomorphism,
2350	  read as an equality of iso-classes,
2351	  \[
2352	    \mathtt{mul\_assoc} \leftarrow \langle\alpha\rangle,
2353	    \quad
2354	    \mathtt{one\_mul} \leftarrow \langle\lambda\rangle,
2355	    \quad
2356	    \mathtt{mul\_one} \leftarrow \langle\rho\rangle,
2357	    \quad
2358	    \mathtt{mul\_comm} \leftarrow \langle\beta\rangle:
2359	  \]
2360	  associativity is \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}, the unit laws are the unitors
2361	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso}, and commutativity is the braiding
2362	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}. No pentagon, triangle or hexagon identity is
2363	  invoked, since each law asserts only \(\mathrm{Nonempty}(\cdots \cong \cdots)\).
2364	  This is the same one-isomorphism-per-law shape that Mathlib's
2365	  \(\mathtt{monoidOfSkeletalMonoidal}\)
2366	  (\texttt{Mathlib/CategoryTheory/Monoidal/Skeleton.lean}) uses on a skeletal
2367	  monoidal category via its skeletality map \(\mathtt{hC}\), but we do \emph{not}
2368	  instantiate that construction: it requires a coherent \([\mathtt{MonoidalCategory}]\)
2369	  (and \([\mathtt{BraidedCategory}]\)) on \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), which is
2370	  exactly the structure for the varying structure sheaf that is unavailable.
2371	  Finally every element is invertible: the dual \(L^{-1}\) of
2372	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible} satisfies \(L \otimes_X L^{-1} \cong
2373	  \mathcal{O}_X\), so \([L^{-1}]\) is a two-sided inverse of \([L]\); equivalently,
2374	  \([L]\) is a unit precisely when \(L\) is \(\otimes\)-invertible
2375	  (\cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}), the inverse witness carried
2376	  existentially by \(\mathtt{IsInvertible}\). This assembles the abelian group ---
2377	  the Picard group of \(X\) (Stacks tag 01CR) --- with \emph{no}
2378	  \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\), \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) or \(\mathtt{Skeleton}\),
2379	  exactly as \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\) carries its \(\mathtt{CommGroup}\) on
2380	  iso-classes of invertible modules (there off a fixed-ring \(\mathtt{Skeleton}\),
2381	  here by hand).
2382	
2383	  Alternatively (fallback), once \cref{lem:jw_ismonoidal} provides the full
2384	  \(\MonoidalCategory\) structure on \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) via
2385	  \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\), the group is the units of the skeleton,
2386	  \(\mathrm{Units}(\mathrm{Skeleton}(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X))\), exactly the
2387	  Mathlib \(\mathtt{Units}(\mathrm{Skeleton}(\cdots))\) template used for
2388	  \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\); this route is not needed for the group law.
2389	\end{proof}

2391	\section{The invertibility-carrier Picard group}
2392	\label{sec:tensorobj_pic_carrier}
2393	
2394	The group law of \cref{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup} is carried on the
2395	\emph{locally-trivial} predicate \(\mathtt{IsLocallyTrivial}\), and on that carrier
2396	the existence of a tensor inverse for each class is an extra obligation
2397	(\cref{lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible}): one must \emph{manufacture} the dual
2398	object \(L^{-1} = \mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_X}(L, \mathcal{O}_X)\) and prove the
2399	contraction \(L \otimes_X L^{-1} \cong \mathcal{O}_X\), which is the whole content of
2400	the dual-infrastructure build of \cref{sec:tensorobj_dual_infra}.
2401	
2402	This section carries the group law on a \emph{different} predicate for which that
2403	obligation evaporates: the \(\otimes\)-invertibility predicate
2404	\(\mathtt{IsInvertible}\) of \cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible},
2405	\[
2406	  \mathtt{IsInvertible}(M)
2407	  \;:\equiv\;
2408	  \exists\, N,\ \mathrm{Nonempty}\bigl(M \otimes_X N \cong \mathcal{O}_X\bigr).
2409	\]
2410	On this carrier the inverse of a class \([M]\) is supplied by the \emph{membership
2411	witness itself} --- the very \(N\) whose existence \(\mathtt{IsInvertible}(M)\)
2412	asserts --- so no inverse object need be constructed and no separate
2413	tensor-inverse lemma is consumed. The cost is shifted to a routine
2414	well-definedness fact (\cref{lem:isinvertible_inverse_welldef}: the witness \(N\) is
2415	determined up to isomorphism), proved from the monoidal coherence isomorphisms
2416	alone. The carrier is the scheme-theoretic analogue of Mathlib's
2417	\(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\), whose elements are exactly the iso-classes of invertible
2418	modules (Stacks tag 01CX).
2419	
2420	\begin{lemma}
2421	[Associator for $\otimes_X$ on invertible objects (corollary of the unconditional
2422	associator)]
2423	  \label{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso_invertible}
2424	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_assoc_iso_invertible}
2425	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, def:scheme_modules_isinvertible,
2426	        lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}
2427	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and let
2428	  \(M, N, P \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) be \(\otimes\)-invertible
2429	  (\cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}). There is an isomorphism
2430	  \[
2431	    (M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X P
2432	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
2433	    M \otimes_X (N \otimes_X P)
2434	  \]
2435	  in \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\). The invertibility-carrier group law
2436	  (\cref{thm:pic_commgroup}) consumes only the \emph{existence} of this
2437	  isomorphism, the proposition \(\mathrm{Nonempty}\bigl((M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X P
2438	  \cong M \otimes_X (N \otimes_X P)\bigr)\); no pentagon, triangle, or naturality
2439	  coherence is ever required.
2440	\end{lemma}
2441	
2442	\begin{proof}
2443	  \uses{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}
2444	  Immediate specialisation of the \emph{unconditional} associator
2445	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso} to the invertible arguments \(M, N, P\); no
2446	  hypothesis on the arguments is used. (The earlier route argued via ``invertible
2447	  \(\Rightarrow\) locally free \(\Rightarrow\) sectionwise flat'' to feed the
2448	  flat-whiskering lemma \cref{lem:flat_whisker_localizer}; that step is \emph{false}
2449	  --- local freeness does not give global sectionwise flatness \(M(U)\) over
2450	  \(\mathcal{O}_X(U)\) for a non-affine open \(U\) --- and is no longer needed, since
2451	  the general associator is itself unconditional, its single open left-whiskering
2452	  obligation \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W} being closed via the d.2
2453	  stalk--tensor commutation \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whiskerleft_via_stalk}.)
2454	\end{proof}
2455	
2456	\begin{definition}
2457	[The invertibility-carrier Picard group \(\Pic X\)]
2458	  \label{def:pic_carrier}
2459	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.PicGroup}
2460	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}
2461	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Tag 01CX (Modules on Ringed Spaces, \S Invertible
2462	  % modules), Definition definition-pic (the Picard group as the abelian group of
2463	  % iso-classes of invertible modules under tensor product)
2464	  % (read from references/stacks-modules.tex, L4350-L4357).
2465	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
2466	  % "\begin{definition}
2467	  % \label{definition-pic}
2468	  % Let $(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$ be a ringed space.
2469	  % The {\it Picard group} $\Pic(X)$ of $X$ is the
2470	  % abelian group whose elements are isomorphism classes of
2471	  % invertible $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules, with addition
2472	  % corresponding to tensor product.
2473	  % \end{definition}"
2474	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Tag 01CX, Definition~\texttt{definition-pic}:
2475	  the Picard group \(\Pic(X)\) is the abelian group of isomorphism classes of
2476	  invertible \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules, with addition the tensor product.}
2477	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. Consider the subtype of \(\otimes\)-invertible objects
2478	  \[
2479	    \mathtt{Invertible}\,X
2480	    \;:=\;
2481	    \bigl\{\, M \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
2482	              \,\bigm|\, \mathtt{IsInvertible}\,M \,\bigr\}
2483	  \]
2484	  of \cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}, and equip it with the setoid whose
2485	  relation is ``there exists an isomorphism'',
2486	  \(M \sim M' :\equiv \mathrm{Nonempty}(M \cong M')\) (a proposition-valued
2487	  equivalence relation: reflexive, symmetric and transitive because isomorphisms
2488	  compose and invert). The \emph{Picard group carrier}
2489	  \[
2490	    \Pic X \;:=\; \mathtt{Invertible}\,X \,/\!\sim
2491	  \]
2492	  is the quotient type of isomorphism classes of \(\otimes\)-invertible
2493	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. We write \([M] \in \Pic X\) for the class of an
2494	  invertible \(M\). This is the scheme-theoretic analogue of Mathlib's
2495	  \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\,R\), carried on \(\mathtt{IsInvertible}\) rather than on
2496	  \(\mathtt{IsLocallyTrivial}\); on a scheme the two predicates agree (Stacks tag
2497	  0B8M), but only the invertibility form carries the inverse witness existentially.
2498	\end{definition}

2500	\begin{lemma}
2501	[\(\otimes\)-invertibility is closed under tensor product]
2502	  \label{lem:isinvertible_tensor}
2503	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.IsInvertible.tensorObj}
2504	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible, lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso_invertible,

2510	  % SOURCE QUOTE:

2526	  Lemma~\texttt{lemma-constructions-invertible} (item 1): the tensor product of two
2527	  invertible \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules is again invertible.}
2528	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(M, M' \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\). If
2529	  \(\mathtt{IsInvertible}\,M\) and \(\mathtt{IsInvertible}\,M'\), then
2530	  \(\mathtt{IsInvertible}(M \otimes_X M')\).
2531	\end{lemma}

2533	\begin{proof}
2534	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible, lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso_invertible,
2535	        lem:tensorobj_comm_iso, lem:tensorobj_unit_iso}
2536	  Let \(N\) be a tensor inverse of \(M\) (so \(M \otimes_X N \cong \mathcal{O}_X\))
2537	  and \(N'\) a tensor inverse of \(M'\) (so \(M' \otimes_X N' \cong \mathcal{O}_X\)),
2538	  furnished by the two invertibility witnesses. We claim \(N \otimes_X N'\) is a
2539	  tensor inverse of \(M \otimes_X M'\). Indeed, reassociating and commuting the four
2540	  factors with the associator \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso_invertible} and the
2541	  braiding \cref{lem:tensorobj_comm_iso},
2542	  \[
2543	    (M \otimes_X M') \otimes_X (N \otimes_X N')
2544	    \;\cong\;
2545	    (M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X (M' \otimes_X N')
2546	    \;\cong\;
2547	    \mathcal{O}_X \otimes_X \mathcal{O}_X
2548	    \;\cong\;
2549	    \mathcal{O}_X,
2550	  \]
2551	  the middle step substituting the two witness isomorphisms and the last being the
2552	  unitor \cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso}. Hence \(N \otimes_X N'\) witnesses
2553	  \(\mathtt{IsInvertible}(M \otimes_X M')\). Every step is consumed only as the
2554	  existence of an isomorphism, so no coherence identity is required.
2555	\end{proof}

1	\chapter{The Quot scheme}
2	\label{chap:Picard_QuotScheme}

5	% STRATEGY NOTE (iter-174). This chapter is the A.2.b sub-row of Route~A
6	% (positive-genus arm of nonempty_jacobianWitness). It packages the

32	\section{Setup and motivation}
33	\label{sec:quot_setup}
34	
35	Throughout this chapter, let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme, \(\pi : X \to S\) a
36	projective morphism, \(L\) a relatively very ample line bundle on \(X\) over \(S\),
37	and \(E\) a coherent sheaf on \(X\). The Quot construction packages the
38	\(T\)-flat coherent quotients \(E_T \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F}\) of the
39	pull-back \(E_T\) to \(X_T = X \times_S T\), modulo the equivalence
40	\(\langle \mathcal{F}, q\rangle = \langle \mathcal{F}', q'\rangle\) defined by
41	\(\ker(q) = \ker(q')\). Fixing the Hilbert polynomial
42	\(\Phi(\lambda) = \chi(\mathcal{F}|_{X_t}(\lambda)) \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\)
43	(constant on connected \(T\) by flatness; see~\)
44	stratifies the Quot functor into pieces \(\Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}\), each of
45	which Grothendieck proved is representable by a projective \(S\)-scheme.
46	
47	% SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1 and
48	% \S 5; FGA Explained Ch.~5, AMS MSM 123 (read from

50	% L2154-L2542). The Hartshorne reference for the polynomial-eventually
51	% property of Euler characteristics is [Hartshorne], III.5.2 (the scanned
52	% PDF at references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf has no text layer, so
53	% the verbatim source-quote in \ is taken from
54	% Nitsure \S 1; Hartshorne III.5.2 is cited in prose as the textbook
55	% reference).

1	\chapter{Flattening Stratification of a Coherent Sheaf}
2	\label{chap:Picard_FlatteningStratification}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/FlatteningStratification.lean
4	
5	% STRATEGY NOTE (iter-174). This chapter is the A.2.a sub-row of Route~A
6	% (positive-genus arm of nonempty_jacobianWitness). It packages the
7	% Nitsure~\S4 flattening-stratification theorem, the technical heart that
8	% turns the absolute Quot-functor representability problem on a projective
9	% morphism \(X \to S\) with coherent input \(\F\) into a finite collection of
10	% locally-closed strata over which \(\F\) becomes flat. Mathlib (master
11	% \texttt{b80f227}) has neither a coherent-sheaf flatness predicate over a
12	% scheme base nor the flattening-stratification construction; this chapter
13	% is the prover-ready specification for the new file
14	% \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/FlatteningStratification.lean} which a
15	% subsequent iter (file-skeleton lane) will scaffold.

20	\section{Setup and motivation}
21	\label{sec:flatstrat_setup}
22	
23	The construction of the Quot scheme \(\Quot_{\F/X/S}^{\Phi,L}\) in Nitsure's exposition
24	of Grothendieck's existence theorem (\cite{nitsure-hilbert-quot}, \S5) proceeds by
25	embedding the functor of points of \(\Quot\) into a Grassmannian via Castelnuovo--Mumford
26	regularity, then cutting out the locus of \emph{quotients with flat fibres of the
27	prescribed Hilbert polynomial} by a closed-subscheme condition.  Cutting out this
28	locus uses, as a primary technical input, the existence of the
29	\emph{flattening stratification} of a coherent sheaf \(\F\) on a projective
30	\(S\)-scheme: a finite collection of locally-closed subschemes of \(S\) on each of which
31	the restriction of \(\F\) becomes flat.
32	
33	This chapter packages the Nitsure~\S4 flattening-stratification theorem as the
34	foundational A.2.a building block. The Quot-scheme construction itself (A.2.b),
35	the relative Picard functor (A.1.c), and the assembly of the Picard scheme
36	representability theorem on a smooth proper curve (A.2.c) live in separate sibling
37	chapters.
38	
39	We work throughout with a noetherian base scheme \(S\), a projective morphism
40	\(\pi : X \to S\), and a coherent \(\OO_X\)-module \(\F\). The conclusion of Nitsure's
41	theorem covers an arbitrary noetherian base with a projective \(X \to S\), which
42	specialises directly to the Route~A consumer setting where \(S = T\) is an arbitrary
43	\(k\)-scheme, \(X = C \times_k T\) for \(C\) a smooth proper curve over the base field
44	\(k\), and \(\F\) is a coherent quotient sheaf produced by the Grassmannian embedding
45	of the Quot functor (cf.\ \ below).
46	
47	\section{Coherent flatness over a base}
48	\label{sec:coh_flat}
49	
50	\begin{definition}
51	
52	\leanok
53	[Coherent sheaf flat over the base]
54	  \label{def:coherent_sheaf_flat}
55	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.CoherentSheafFlat}
56	  Let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme, \(f : X \to S\) a morphism of finite type, and
57	  \(\F\) a coherent \(\OO_X\)-module.  We say \(\F\) is \emph{flat over \(S\)}
58	  (equivalently, \(\F\) is \emph{\(f\)-flat}, or \emph{\(S\)-flat}) if for every
59	  point \(x \in X\) the stalk \(\F_x\) is a flat \(\OO_{S, f(x)}\)-module, where the
60	  \(\OO_{S, f(x)}\)-module structure is given by the composite
61	  \(\OO_{S, f(x)} \to \OO_{X, x} \to \End(\F_x)\).
62	  \textit{Source: cf.\ [Stacks Project], tag 00HB (flat modules); [Nitsure], \S4
63	  passim.}
64	  Equivalently (after pulling back to an affine open \(U = \Spec A \subseteq S\) and
65	  an affine open \(V = \Spec B \subseteq f^{-1}(U)\) with \(\F|_V = \widetilde{M}\)
66	  for \(M\) a finite \(B\)-module), \(\F|_V\) is \(\OO_U\)-flat in the sense that \(M\) is a
67	  flat \(A\)-module (with the \(A\)-module structure coming from the ring map
68	  \(A \to B\)).
69	\end{definition}
70	
71	\begin{remark}
72	\label{rem:coh_flat_pullback}
73	  The condition is stable under base change: for any morphism \(g : S' \to S\),
74	  pulling back along the cartesian square
75	  \[
76	    \begin{tikzcd}
77	      X' \ar[r, "g'"] \ar[d, "f'"'] & X \ar[d, "f"] \\
78	      S' \ar[r, "g"]                & S
79	    \end{tikzcd}
80	  \]

17	This chapter introduces the project's named declaration for the M2.a sub-step of the genus-\(0\) Albanese-witness argument: rigidity of morphisms from a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve of genus \(0\) to a smooth proper geometrically irreducible group scheme, \textbf{over an algebraically closed base field \(\bar k\) of characteristic zero} (the alg-closed pivot of \S "Iter-152 alg-closed pivot" below; descent of the constancy conclusion to a general base field \(k\) is a separate downstream step, hosted in the genus-\(0\) witness chapter). The classical statement (Mumford, \emph{Abelian Varieties}, Chapter~II~\S4) is
18	
19	\begin{quote}
20	  \emph{Let \(A\) be an abelian variety of positive dimension over an algebraically closed field \(\bar k\). Then every morphism \(f \colon \mathbb P^1_{\bar k} \to A\) is constant.}
21	\end{quote}
22	
23	This is the keystone classical input for the C.2.d sub-step of Jacobian.tex \S C.2: the genus-\(0\) universal-property step of \cref{thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness} that concludes the universal-pointed-morphism factorisation by ruling out positive-dimensional images of \(f \colon C \to A\) when \(C\) has genus \(0\) and a \(k\)-rational marked point.
24	
25	\paragraph{Disposition (iter-155): \cref{thm:rigidity_over_kbar} is a gated NAMED GAP.} The named declaration \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.rigidity\_over\_kbar} is landed with a \texttt{sorry} body and is held as a \textbf{named gap} in the same family as \cref{thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness} (Jacobian.tex) and the deferred Serre-duality / Riemann--Roch stack --- honestly disclosed, not on a prover lane. The substantive content (the C.2.d sub-step below, ruling out a \(1\)-dimensional image of \(f\)) reduces to the vanishing of the cotangent pull-back \(\mathrm df \colon f^{*}\Omega_{A/\bar k} \to \Omega_{C/\bar k}\), and a decisive iter-155 \texttt{mathlib-analogist} consult (\texttt{analogies/df-zero-production-iter155.md}) established that \textbf{producing \(\mathrm df = 0\) is irreducibly a global-sections fact}, gated on two genuinely-absent inputs:
26	
27	\begin{itemize}
28	  \item \textbf{(gap i) Global cotangent-triviality of \(A\):} \(\Omega_{A/\bar k} \cong \mathcal O_A^{\oplus g}\), generated by translation-invariant differentials. The presheaf globalisation that would build this (\texttt{mulRight\_globalises\_cotangent}) was \textbf{excised from the Lean tree at iter-145}; \texttt{GrpObj.cotangentSpaceAtIdentity} \(+\) \texttt{shearMulRight} survive only as a \emph{design template}. Estimated \(\sim 800\)--\(1500\) LOC. Mathlib's only ``group cotangent is trivial'' idiom is the smooth-manifold \texttt{GroupLieAlgebra} stack, which does not port to schemes.
29	  \item \textbf{(gap ii) Serre duality \(H^0(C, \Omega_{C/\bar k}) = 0\)} for the genus-\(0\) curve. Given the global trivialisation of gap (i), \(\mathrm df \in H^0\bigl(C, \mathcal{H}om(f^{*}\Omega_{A/\bar k}, \Omega_{C/\bar k})\bigr) \cong \bigl(H^0(C, \Omega_{C/\bar k})\bigr)^{\oplus g}\), so \(\mathrm df = 0 \iff H^0(C, \Omega_{C/\bar k}) = 0\). Mathlib snapshot \texttt{b80f227} has no dualizing sheaf and no Riemann--Roch; the project's \texttt{Cohomology/*} stack computes \(H^1(C, \mathcal O_C)\) (genus) but has \textbf{no \(H^0\)-vanishing lemma and no genus\(\leftrightarrow\Omega\) bridge}. The genus-\(0\) hypothesis \(H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) = 0\) does \emph{not} yield \(H^0(C, \Omega_{C/\bar k}) = 0\) without the duality. Estimated \(\sim 3000\)--\(8000\) LOC.
30	\end{itemize}
31	
32	\noindent The chart-algebra envelope of \cref{sec:RigidityKbar_shared_pile} (\cref{lem:KaehlerDifferential_mem_range_algebraMap_of_D_eq_zero} (KDM), \cref{lem:constants_integral_over_base_field}, and the delegate \cref{lem:chart_algebra_df_zero_factors_through_constant_on_chart}), which \emph{is} closed and axiom-clean, supplies only the \textbf{converse} half of the bridge: ``if \(\mathrm df = 0\) then \(f\) factors through \(\Spec \bar k\) (is constant)''. It \emph{cannot} produce \(\mathrm df = 0\): on each affine chart \(V \subseteq C\), \(\Omega_{C/\bar k}(V)\) is free of rank \(1\) (nonzero), so a chart-by-chart computation never detects the vanishing --- the vanishing materialises only on global sections. Earlier framings in this chapter (the piece-(iv) ``DEFERRED, not on the C.2.d critical path'' disposition and the ``No-Serre-duality layer'') asserted that the chart-algebra route \emph{avoids} Serre duality; \textbf{that is false}, and is corrected throughout \cref{sec:RigidityKbar_shared_pile}: \(H^0(C, \Omega_{C/\bar k}) = 0\) is \emph{on} the critical path for producing \(\mathrm df = 0\), not avoided.
33	
34	The project does \textbf{not} open a ``produce \(\mathrm df = 0\)'' prover lane until one of two candidate closure routes is built; the decision between them is a leading open strategic question and the chapter commits to neither:
35	\begin{itemize}
36	  \item \textbf{Route (a):} build gaps (i) \(+\) (ii) directly.
37	  \item \textbf{Route (b):} the dual-abelian-variety keystone --- ``morphisms \(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k} \to A\) are constant because \(\Pic^0(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}) = 0\)''. Earlier rejected as ``needs \(\Pic^0\)'', but since the positive-genus arm of \cref{thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness} (Route A) needs the \(\Pic^0\) engine \emph{regardless}, route (b) may amortise the shared machinery and be globally cheaper than a standalone Serre-duality build.
38	\end{itemize}
39	
40	\paragraph{Iter-152 alg-closed pivot.} The project committed an architectural pivot: rigidity is proved \textbf{only over an algebraically closed base field \(\bar k\) of characteristic zero}, and the constancy conclusion descends to a general base field \(k\) downstream. The reason is that the chart-algebra core lemma \cref{lem:KaehlerDifferential_mem_range_algebraMap_of_D_eq_zero} (KDM) is mathematically \emph{false} as a bare \(B\)-only algebra lemma: a prover lane exhibited two counterexamples that satisfy every hypothesis yet have \(\ker D \supsetneq \operatorname{range}(\mathtt{algebraMap})\) --- namely \(B = k \times k\) (finite \'etale over \(k\)) and \(B = \mathbb Q(\sqrt 2)\) over \(\mathbb Q\). The missing content is precisely ``\(k\) algebraically closed in \(B\)''. Adding the \emph{joint} hypotheses \texttt{[IsAlgClosed kbar]} \(+\) \texttt{[IsDomain B]} makes it true: \texttt{[IsDomain B]} kills the \(B = k \times k\) counterexample and \texttt{[IsAlgClosed kbar]} kills the \(\mathbb Q(\sqrt 2)/\mathbb Q\) counterexample. (Neither hypothesis suffices alone; the iter-151 note that ``\texttt{[IsDomain B]} alone fails'' considered the hypotheses only one-at-a-time.) Over an algebraically closed base the \cref{lem:constants_integral_over_base_field} constants lemma also collapses to a one-liner via \texttt{IsAlgClosed.algebraMap\_bijective\_of\_isIntegral}, and the entire (S3.\textsc{sep}/\textsc{pi}) base-change-to-\(\bar k\) chain is descoped from the M2.a critical path.

The alg-closed pivot supersedes that framing for the rigidity theorem itself: rigidity is established over \(\bar k\), and the descent of the constancy conclusion to a general \(k\) is a short downstream consequence of \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Flat.epi\_of\_flat\_of\_surjective} (faithfully-flat covers are epimorphisms of schemes), with the detailed descent living in the genus-\(0\) witness chapter. The group-scheme cotangent-triviality pieces (i)/(iii) below remain genuinely intrinsic to the group scheme and are stated over a general base where that is sound; only the curve-side \(\mathrm df = 0 \Rightarrow\) constancy chain (the KDM/constants core) requires the alg-closed base.
